name: VA Clothing Allowance
description: Annual payment for veterans whose service-connected condition or related medication causes wear or damage to clothing. Many eligible veterans never apply.
eligibility: Veterans with service-connected conditions that damage clothing, or who use prosthetic/orthopedic devices that cause clothing wear
why_missed: Most veterans don't realize clothing damage from skin conditions, prosthetics, or medications qualifies
how_to_claim: Apply through VA Form 10-8678 or contact your local VA Prosthetics department

name: VA Automobile Adaptive Equipment Grant
description: One-time grant to purchase or adapt a vehicle for veterans with qualifying service-connected disabilities. Separate from the automobile allowance already listed in partner resources.
eligibility: Veterans with loss of or permanent loss of use of hands or feet, or permanent impairment of vision in both eyes
why_missed: Often confused with the separate automobile allowance; this is the ADAPTIVE EQUIPMENT portion that covers modifications
how_to_claim: Apply through VA Form 10-1394 at your VA medical center
annual_value: Up to $22,563 (2025 rate, one-time)

name: Veterans Mortgage Life Insurance (VMLI)
description: Mortgage life insurance for severely disabled veterans who have received Specially Adapted Housing grants. Covers mortgage balance up to $200,000 upon death.
eligibility: Veterans who have received a Specially Adapted Housing (SAH) or Special Housing Adaptation (SHA) grant
why_missed: Only available to SAH/SHA grant recipients; many don't know it exists even after receiving the housing grant
how_to_claim: Contact VA Insurance Center at 1-800-669-8477
annual_value: Up to $200,000 mortgage coverage

name: Service-Disabled Veterans Insurance (S-DVI / RH Insurance)
description: Life insurance for veterans with service-connected disabilities. Basic coverage of $10,000 with supplemental up to $30,000. Must apply within 2 years of receiving a new service-connected rating.
eligibility: Veterans with a new service-connected disability rating, within 2 years of notification
why_missed: Strict 2-year application window after rating notification; most veterans miss the deadline because they don't know about it
how_to_claim: Apply via VA Form 29-4364 within 2 years of receiving service-connected disability notification
annual_value: $10,000 basic + up to $30,000 supplemental

name: Veterans' Group Life Insurance (VGLI) Conversion
description: Servicemembers can convert SGLI to VGLI within 240 days of separation without health screening. After 240 days, a health questionnaire is required. Many veterans miss the no-screening window.
eligibility: All separating service members within 240 days of discharge for guaranteed coverage (1 year 120 days with health screen)
why_missed: The 240-day guaranteed-issue window is not well publicized during transition; premiums increase with age so early conversion saves money
how_to_claim: Apply through OSGLI at 1-800-419-1473 or online at benefits.va.gov/insurance
annual_value: Up to $500,000 coverage

name: VA Dental Eligibility — Service-Connected Conditions
description: Veterans with any service-connected dental condition or rated 100% disabled are eligible for comprehensive VA dental care. Most veterans incorrectly believe dental is never covered.
eligibility: Veterans with service-connected dental conditions, 100% rated veterans, former POWs, or those with dental conditions aggravating service-connected medical conditions
why_missed: Widespread misconception that VA never covers dental; eligibility depends on specific conditions most veterans don't know to check
how_to_claim: Apply through VA enrollment or contact your VAMC dental clinic directly
annual_value: Full dental coverage (cleanings, restorations, prosthetics)

name: VA Foreign Medical Program (FMP)
description: Pays for treatment of service-connected conditions for veterans living or traveling abroad. Most veterans abroad don't know VA will reimburse their overseas care.
eligibility: Veterans with VA-rated service-connected conditions who receive treatment outside the US
why_missed: Most veterans assume all VA benefits end at the US border; FMP allows reimbursement for overseas care of SC conditions
how_to_claim: Register with VA FMP at 303-331-7590 or email FMP@va.gov, submit claims within 1 year of treatment
annual_value: Reimbursement for all SC-related treatment abroad

name: Civilian Health and Medical Program (CHAMPVA)
description: Healthcare coverage for spouses and children of veterans who are permanently and totally disabled or who died from service-connected conditions. Often confused with TRICARE.
eligibility: Spouse and children of veterans rated permanently and totally disabled, or deceased veterans whose death was service-connected
why_missed: Families confuse CHAMPVA with TRICARE and assume they're ineligible; CHAMPVA has no enrollment fee and covers most medical services
how_to_claim: Apply via VA Form 10-10d to VHA Office of Community Care, CHAMPVA
annual_value: Comprehensive health coverage (75% of allowable charges after $50 deductible)

name: VA Beneficiary Travel Reimbursement
description: Mileage reimbursement for travel to VA medical appointments. Many eligible veterans never submit claims because they don't know the benefit exists.
eligibility: Veterans with 30%+ service-connected rating, receiving VA pension, traveling for SC-related treatment, or with income below threshold
why_missed: Requires submitting a claim after each appointment; most veterans don't know to ask at the travel office or submit online via BTSSS
how_to_claim: Submit via Beneficiary Travel Self Service System (BTSSS) online or at VA medical center travel office within 30 days of appointment
annual_value: $0.415/mile (2025 rate) + tolls and parking

name: Combat Zone Tax Exclusion (CZTE)
description: Military pay earned while serving in a combat zone is partially or fully excluded from federal income tax. Many veterans don't retroactively claim missed exclusions.
eligibility: Service members who served in designated combat zones during the tax year
why_missed: Retroactive claims for missed combat zone exclusions can go back 3 years; many don't file amended returns
how_to_claim: File IRS Form 1040-X to amend prior returns; contact a VITA volunteer tax preparer at your VA or military installation
annual_value: Varies — can be thousands in refunds for missed exclusions

name: Disability Pay Federal Tax Exemption
description: VA disability compensation is completely exempt from federal and state income tax. Some veterans don't realize this applies to ALL VA disability pay, not just higher ratings.
eligibility: All veterans receiving VA disability compensation at any rating
why_missed: Some veterans mistakenly report disability pay as income; others don't claim state exemptions that piggyback off the federal exemption
how_to_claim: Ensure VA disability income is NOT reported as taxable income on federal/state returns; consult a tax professional if previously reported
annual_value: Tax savings of 10-37% of disability compensation depending on bracket

name: Property Tax Exemption for Disabled Veterans
description: Most states offer partial or full property tax exemptions for disabled veterans. Exemption amounts vary dramatically by state and rating percentage.
eligibility: Varies by state — typically veterans with 10%+ VA rating; some states require 100% for full exemption
why_missed: Veterans must proactively apply at their county assessor's office; exemptions don't auto-apply even after establishing VA rating
how_to_claim: Contact your county tax assessor or use your state's veteran property tax exemption application
annual_value: Varies: $500/year (low rating, some states) to 100% exemption (100% rated, TX/FL/others)

name: State Veteran Bonus Payments
description: Many states offer one-time cash bonuses to veterans who served during specific conflicts or deployments. Some states have bonuses veterans never claimed.
eligibility: Varies by state — typically requires residency and qualifying service during specific periods (Iraq, Afghanistan, etc.)
why_missed: Each state has different eligibility windows and application processes; many veterans don't know their state offers this
how_to_claim: Contact your State Department of Veterans Affairs; some states require residency at time of service
annual_value: One-time: $100-$5,000+ depending on state and deployment

name: Free National Park Lifetime Pass (America the Beautiful)
description: Free lifetime access to all national parks, wildlife refuges, and other federal recreation areas. Available to all veterans and Gold Star families since 2020.
eligibility: All veterans with DD-214, veteran health ID (VHIC), veteran designation on state-issued ID, or VA benefit verification letter
why_missed: Many veterans don't know the free pass was expanded from disabled-only to ALL veterans in 2020
how_to_claim: Present valid veteran ID at any national park entrance station or request online
annual_value: $80/year savings (cost of the standard annual pass)

name: State Hunting & Fishing License Exemptions
description: Many states offer free or reduced-cost hunting and fishing licenses for disabled veterans or all veterans. Requirements vary significantly by state.
eligibility: Varies by state — some require disabled veteran status, others extend to all veterans with state residency
why_missed: Must be requested separately from standard license purchase; not automatically applied
how_to_claim: Contact your state wildlife/fish & game department or apply at license vendor with veteran ID/DD-214
annual_value: $20-$150/year in license savings depending on state

name: Veterans Preference in Federal Hiring
description: Veterans receive 5 or 10 point preference in federal job hiring. Disabled veterans receive additional preference and some are eligible for non-competitive appointment.
eligibility: All honorably discharged veterans (5 points); disabled veterans, Purple Heart recipients, and campaign badge holders (10 points)
why_missed: Many veterans don't claim preference on USAJobs applications or don't understand how to document it properly
how_to_claim: Claim on USAJobs application; attach DD-214 and SF-15 (for 10-point preference); VA disability letter if applicable
annual_value: Competitive advantage — can be the difference between getting hired or not

name: Vocational Rehabilitation Chapter 31 Subsistence Allowance
description: Monthly subsistence allowance paid during VR&E participation, ON TOP of GI Bill or disability pay. Many eligible veterans use GI Bill instead without comparing.
eligibility: Veterans with 20%+ service-connected rating and an employment handicap; 10% with serious employment handicap
why_missed: Veterans default to GI Bill without evaluating VR&E, which may cover more expenses and doesn't count against GI Bill months
how_to_claim: Apply via VA.gov or VA Form 28-1900; attend orientation and counseling with VR&E counselor
annual_value: $735-$1,105/month subsistence (2025, varies by training type and dependents) plus tuition, books, supplies

name: Survivors' and Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA Chapter 35) Transfer
description: Education benefits for dependents and survivors of veterans who are permanently and totally disabled or who died from service-connected causes. Up to 36 months of benefits.
eligibility: Spouses and children of veterans with permanent and total service-connected disability or who died of service-connected causes
why_missed: Families often don't realize they have independent education benefits separate from GI Bill transfer
how_to_claim: Dependent applies via VA Form 22-5490; must be between ages 18-26 for children (extensions available)
annual_value: $1,298/month (2025 full-time institutional rate) for up to 36 months

name: SCRA Interest Rate Cap (6%)
description: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act caps interest rates at 6% on pre-service debt for active duty. Many veterans don't retroactively claim refunds for overcharges during service.
eligibility: Active duty service members with debt incurred BEFORE entering active duty
why_missed: Lenders don't automatically apply the cap; service members must request it in writing with military orders
how_to_claim: Send written request with copy of military orders to each lender; request retroactive refund if overcharged
annual_value: Can save hundreds to thousands per year on mortgages, student loans, credit cards

name: TSP Matching and Roth Contribution for Combat Pay
description: Service members can contribute tax-exempt combat zone pay to Roth TSP for triple tax advantage: no tax in, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawal. The $23,500 limit is separate from the $69,000 total limit.
eligibility: Service members receiving tax-exempt combat zone pay
why_missed: Many service members don't maximize Roth TSP contributions during deployment; the triple tax advantage is the best deal in military finance
how_to_claim: Adjust TSP contributions via myPay before or during deployment; elect Roth contributions for combat zone pay
annual_value: Potentially tens of thousands in tax-free retirement savings over career

name: Free Tax Preparation (VITA / MilTax)
description: Free tax preparation services specifically for military and veterans through VITA (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance) and MilTax by Military OneSource.
eligibility: All active duty, Guard/Reserve, and veterans (VITA has income limits; MilTax has none for military)
why_missed: Many veterans pay for tax preparation without knowing about free services specifically for them
how_to_claim: MilTax: militaryonesource.mil/financial-legal/tax-resource-center; VITA: IRS.gov/VITA or call 211
annual_value: $50-$400 savings on tax prep fees

name: VA Home Loan Funding Fee Exemption
description: Veterans with service-connected disabilities are exempt from the VA home loan funding fee. This saves thousands on home purchases and refinances.
eligibility: Veterans receiving VA disability compensation, surviving spouses receiving DIC, and active duty Purple Heart recipients
why_missed: Some veterans pay the funding fee at closing and must request a retroactive refund after receiving their disability rating
how_to_claim: Provide Certificate of Eligibility showing exempt status; if already paid, request refund through VA Regional Loan Center
annual_value: One-time savings of $4,000-$15,000+ depending on loan amount and down payment

name: Post-9/11 GI Bill BAH at 100% Rate
description: The Post-9/11 GI Bill pays Basic Allowance for Housing at E-5 with dependents rate based on the school's ZIP code. Online-only students receive 50% of the national average.
eligibility: Veterans with 100% Post-9/11 GI Bill eligibility (36+ months active duty after 9/10/2001)
why_missed: Many veterans don't realize attending at least one in-person class qualifies for the full (often much higher) local BAH rate instead of the 50% online rate
how_to_claim: Enroll in at least one in-person class to qualify for full BAH rate at school's ZIP code
annual_value: $12,000-$48,000/year depending on location (in-person vs. online rate difference can be $6,000-$24,000/year)

name: State Vehicle Registration Fee Waivers
description: Many states waive or reduce vehicle registration fees and offer free veteran license plates for disabled veterans. Some extend to all veterans.
eligibility: Varies by state — typically disabled veterans; some states extend to all veterans for standard plates
why_missed: Must be requested at DMV; not automatically applied even with veteran designation on license
how_to_claim: Apply at your local DMV with DD-214 and VA rating letter; request veteran plates and fee waiver simultaneously
annual_value: $30-$200/year in registration savings; free plates save $15-$50

name: Free State Park Passes for Veterans
description: Many states offer free or discounted annual state park passes for veterans or disabled veterans. Not the same as the federal national park pass.
eligibility: Varies by state — some require disability; others extend to all veterans; a few include active duty
why_missed: Separate from federal national park pass; must be requested from state parks department
how_to_claim: Contact your state parks department or apply at park entrance with veteran ID/DD-214
annual_value: $20-$100/year in day-use and camping fee savings

name: SBP-DIC Offset Elimination (2023)
description: As of January 2023, the SBP-DIC offset was fully eliminated. Surviving spouses now receive BOTH Survivor Benefit Plan and Dependency and Indemnity Compensation without reduction.
eligibility: Surviving spouses eligible for both SBP (military retirement) and DIC (VA survivor benefit)
why_missed: The offset was phased out over 3 years (2021-2023); some survivors haven't reclaimed their SBP because they don't know the offset ended
how_to_claim: Contact DFAS at 1-800-321-1080 to verify SBP payments are restored; contact VA at 1-800-827-1000 for DIC
annual_value: Up to $1,612/month in DIC + full SBP annuity (combined can exceed $3,000/month)

name: VA Commissary & Exchange Access for All Veterans
description: Since January 2020, all veterans with service-connected disabilities and Purple Heart recipients can shop at military commissaries and exchanges. Access expanded to all honorably discharged veterans for online exchange shopping.
eligibility: In-person: veterans with SC disability or Purple Heart. Online: all honorably discharged veterans via shopmyexchange.com
why_missed: Many veterans believe commissary/exchange access ended at separation; the 2020 expansion is not widely known
how_to_claim: Obtain a Veteran Health Identification Card (VHIC) or use VHIC/VA ID at commissary; shop online at shopmyexchange.com with veteran verification
annual_value: $1,000-$4,000/year in savings on groceries and retail (commissary prices average 23.7% below commercial)

name: Toll Road Exemptions for Disabled Veterans
description: Several states offer free or reduced toll road access for disabled veterans with veteran license plates. Available in Texas, Florida, New York, and other states.
eligibility: Varies by state — typically 100% disabled veterans with state veteran plates; some states extend to lower ratings
why_missed: Requires specific veteran plates AND registration with the toll authority; not automatic even with plates
how_to_claim: Register veteran plates with your state toll authority (e.g., TxDOT, FDOT, NY Thruway); process varies by state
annual_value: $200-$2,000+/year depending on commute and state

name: VA Specially Adapted Housing (SAH) — Second Grant
description: Veterans who received an initial SAH grant may qualify for a second grant up to $40,637 for a different home. The lifetime maximum is three uses totaling $109,986.
eligibility: Veterans who previously used SAH grant and need to modify a different primary residence
why_missed: Veterans assume SAH is one-time only; up to 3 uses are allowed with different grant caps per use
how_to_claim: Apply through VA Form 26-4555 for subsequent SAH grant
annual_value: Up to $40,637 second grant / $109,986 lifetime total

name: Veteran Readiness and Employment (VR&E) Self-Employment Track
description: VR&E Chapter 31 includes a self-employment track that provides funding for business plans, equipment, supplies, and initial inventory for veteran entrepreneurs.
eligibility: Veterans eligible for VR&E (20%+ rating with employment handicap) pursuing self-employment as a vocational goal
why_missed: Most veterans only know VR&E for education/training; the self-employment track is rarely promoted by VR&E counselors
how_to_claim: Discuss self-employment track with VR&E counselor; develop business plan as part of rehabilitation plan
annual_value: Equipment, supplies, and startup costs covered; subsistence allowance during plan development
